speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, September 20, 1995. Bluma Weinstock.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I make a mistake when I went to sanatorium, was in 1947. I was two years there. And being in-- and I got already two children. One is Esther. One is Gitta. And Esther was two and a half year old, and Gitta was just maybe two months or the less. And, and I stopped to bleed a lot, and I didn't-- I even remembered that time that I was sick in Auschwitz, in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to a doctor, to German doctor, and the doctor said to me, probably it's from your nose, because I looked good. And he give me some medicine. Didn't help. And I start to bleeding even more and more. I start to spit with-- with blood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the doctor said, you know, you better go to another doctor. An X-ray doctor, he should check your lungs. So I went to the-- to the other doctor. He check-- he check me. He said I have double [INAUDIBLE] on both lungs. They're open. And you have to go to a sanatorium. You cannot go back home, because, you know, your children, your husband, can get sick, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was crying, and-- and-- I said, how I can-- I have a child from Joseph a few weeks old, and I have to go away? [SIGHS] So we-- we went to a sanatorium. This was in Regensburg. We lived in Schwandorf. It was a big sanatorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't want to even hear what the doctor said. We wasn't sure that he's telling us the truth. So I went to the sanatorium. And he looked my lungs. He said, you stay better here right away. Don't go away. Because your lungs is just terrible. Not good. And I went home, and I-- that time, I hear they have a [INAUDIBLE] that-- [INAUDIBLE] and maybe I not going to be-- I didn't want to stay just in the German sanatorium. I want to stay with-- from the refugees, like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in. This was 1947. I stayed there. And then-- I didn't have-- I said that I went home. Now I have to leave my children. And-- and I came to sanatorium. The doctor said, you have to lay. I have to lay. And-- and they check me. Every day they check of you, positive or negative.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, the whole time, negative. And they-- they didn't want to give me no medication. Just they said it's good to, I don't know, to drink duck fat. My husband brought me duck fat. He brought me the egg shell. They make like a-- I should-- they make like a cream. And I did. I tried everything what possible. I want to go home and be healthy to-- to my family, to my children, because this was everything what-- what we got that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was taking care of your children at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, we got-- he took a maid in. And he couldn't-- we lived in Schwandorf. And he couldn't-- she didn't take care good for the kids. And the-- then he came to me, visiting. He said, we have no choice. We have to give the children to a-- a children home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- he-- they look-- was looking for a good children home. They went to Herrsching. It was called that. And then Esther, she was two and a half, and they stayed there two years. And I-- I couldn't see them. Just I begged sometimes the doctor, he should allowed me to go. I could see them for a window, to see in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I remember the-- that Gitta, my little one, she didn't want to come. Then-- then I said, and I was in sanatorium for the whole time, I said, it's no end. I don't know what's gonna be. I laying and laying, and they said that my lungs are open. I spit with blood. Then they told me they have sanatorium in Germany. Forgot the name. Anyway, I went there. And he was one from the biggest professors from lung, and I-- he checked me out. And he said to me like this-- I went a few times-- you have to go, and they have to give you medicine. If you not [INAUDIBLE] medicine, you-- it's worse and get every time worser. Even you not-- even you not positive. But you-- you sick. Your lungs are not good. And he said, you-- he suggest that they should give me streptomycin. And-- and it was a medicine they called, I think, [NON-ENGLISH] from-- from Switzerland. And they start to give me this. And I start to feel-- to get better. And they X-rayed me every day, and they said they getting better. The shots was very terrible shots. They give you-- you get black. And the nurses have to put on eyeglasses, [INAUDIBLE] they could get blind. So I took those shot, maybe a [INAUDIBLE]. And [INAUDIBLE]. Then they let me out. And I felt-- and they said-- and I went back to that professor. Heidelberg was the sanatorium. Heidelberg. And I went back and he checked me. He said to me, you looked healed. You can go. You don't have to stay in sanatorium. And I came back to the-- and I said, I-- they said I have to sign myself if I want to go out. They don't want to let me out. And I signed off myself, that I want to go out. In that time, we bought home first my oldest, Esther, from the children home. And maybe two months later, we took Gitta. And she [INAUDIBLE], I remember, we went to pick her up, she-- she know, then, I have pictures with the nurse. I could-- And she didn't want to go to me. She didn't know me. [? I mean, ?] it took a short time. She know who her mother is, and her father.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you all come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- yeah, we had [INAUDIBLE]. They took my husband right away. Me to [INAUDIBLE] because they saw-- they ask me if I was in a sanatorium, or where, because they didn't want to bring some-- they are afraid, TB, to the-- to the United States. Then they said that-- they-- they check me. I went all the-- let's see-- every month, I went to the-- to the American. They check. And they said that isn't changed. They said, OK. They let me go to-- to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your trip, coming here to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came not [? very-- ?] in a boat. In the boat, we were so sick. And, you know, it was-- it was like that. The woman was separately, and the husband was separately. We-- we wasn't together. And my children was so sick. And me, I was thinking we not-- we not gonna see the United States no more, from the-- from the boat. And he was laying sick, too. In the end, he start to feel better. And I-- we went-- it was about two weeks, we was on the boat. Was very long.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you come? Where did you settle?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to New Orleans. And we-- I supposed to go to Long Beach, California. Our contract was there. Because that time was the Joint in Germany, and he knew-- they knew that I was sick. And they wanted-- they said, you should have a better climate. And my brother was already here in Milwaukee, and he said, you-- my sister's coming. So we came to New Orleans. He said, you cannot go to Long Beach. You have to go to Milwaukee. So we came to Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Esther, Gitta, and Rosalie. One is born in United States, in Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were raising your children, did you talk to them about your experiences during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, that what I told you that story, they don't know the whole story. I told them some, you know. They ask me. I-- I never sit down and told them that story like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about it all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. I think all the time. And beside this, I have very bad dreams all the time. I'm hiding. I'm running. I'm afraid. I'm crying in the night. My husband wakes me up. He said I cried. In the morning, I tell my kids, oy, I got a bad dream. I'm again in concentration camp. Yeah, you cannot forget this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have 10 grandchildren. Beautiful grandchildren. Three already in college. One is in St. Louis, Washington University. One started the now, Duke University. And one is in Harvard University. And they're wonderful. They're wonderful children, grandchildren. I love them. They love me. And I have beautiful three son-in-laws. I couldn't ask better sons if they were mine. They're wonderful. Two are-- two are lawyers, and one is a professor. My older daughter is a professor, too. She teached here in university.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they bring you a lot of happiness.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our best time in life. Just we should feel better. I have problems with my heart. And other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult for you to make this tape today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very difficult. You know, I-- they're doing this in Milwaukee, too. And since I'm here, so many years, 40 years, they want to for me. And I always said to [INAUDIBLE], I always said, nah. I said another time, another time. I never could do it. Now the kids are saying, you have to do it for your children, for your grandchildren. I don't know. It's [? not ?] perfect. You know, my English it's not so perfect. And it's hard to remember, too. It's so many years. It's 50 years. It's not easy. I'm glad I did it. They have some papers. We was in [INAUDIBLE]. I talked a little bit, not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any last message that you have, either for your children and grandchildren, who will be seeing this tape, or a message to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should not happen again. Never. And they should know. I should have go through-- what-- what I went through, they should have happiness for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you, too. This is my father, Hershel. This was before the war, took before the war, yes. I think he was-- he was 49 when he went to Auschwitz, the crematorium. This is my father, my stepmother, and my little sister. My father's [INAUDIBLE], Hershel. And her name is Fella. And this is my little sister, [? Sarah. ?] This was in Sosnowiec. Probably like a Saturday evening, they took a walk. This is my brother, [? Moishe. ?] He was in the army. This is before the war. He didn't survive. He got killed-- he was in the army. He got killed in the army, in Poland. This is my sister, Chanaleh, my stepsister. I loved her so much. I-- I raised them. This was in Poland, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She did not so. She-- she went to Auschwitz. This-- this I am, and this is my stepmother's sister, before the war. She went to Israel. And she lived-- married in Israel. She went before the war to Israel. This is my brother, from Buchenwald. He was in Buchenwald. And this is his friend. And I think this is-- this must be after the war, in Buchenwald, right away after the war. This is, on the right, this is my brother, Jack. And he survived from Buchenwald. This is our wedding in Schwandorf. And there's two brothers, three brothers, my husband's, and a sister-law, and some friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '46, in Schwandorf. 1946. We married. After the war. This is-- this is when I was in sanatorium [INAUDIBLE]. And this is some friends, [? what ?] we was laying in bed together. I am the middle. In the middle, I am. This is my first daughter. This is my first daughter. When she was born, I just thinking my whole world came to me, because I want to have a daughter and give my mother's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther. This is my all three lovely daughters. Esther's in the-- on the right. Gitta in the middle. And Rosie the left. And Rosie's born in the United States, and they both are born in Germany. The first [INAUDIBLE] in the right is Tamar, Marissa, Devorah, Robbie, Lauren, Ilana, Mara, Sari--

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Alex, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aaron.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aaron. Aaron, in the last.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these are?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren, which I am proud of them. They're the nicest kids. This is my husband, Pincus, which I love him more, [? 220 ?]. He's the best husband what can get. I can do it, and everything, he's-- and I-- he always say I raised-- I raised the best children. And I love him very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love Bluma. She's a beautiful wife, a good wife. She's a good mother to the children. And I know she got good taste. She picked me. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in my number. And I came the first day we came to Auschwitz. It's 66632. It's right? Yeah?
